OURRENT AFFAIRB,

The members of the Technical Committee of Water-
ford are about to appoint a head master to the new
Technical Schools in that city. We understand that
the vacancy was advertised in Britain; how far it was
advertised in Ireland wo do not know ; certainly it was
not advertised in the Leapgr. The number of appli-
cants for the vacancy is closo on a hundred, and of
these, all but about five are non-Irish—if it be safe to
judge by the names. We hope that tho members of the
Commiftee will seo, as far as possible, that full atben-
tion is given to the applications from Trishmen. It will
be remembered that Sir Horace Plunkett, in referring,
during his recent evidence given at the Inquiry, to
what he called the “ abuse’’ to which the Departmont
was treated on the head of its importations, said:—
« And it was curious that the fact that most of theso
aliens were appointed by the local representative bodies,
the Department, merely passing on their qualifications,
did not serve to lessen the abuse of the central body for
their importations.” Now wo hear that all the names
have been forwarded to the Department (Scotch), and
that the task of that institution is to select five

What is to prevent the

!

Our confidence in the Department (Scotch) is not un-
limited.

. By tho way, we hear also that Limerick is advertis-
ing in Britain, in a paper called Nature, for a librarian
at a salary of £110 plus house, etc.

According to tho Freeman, the patriots who voted
for, amongst other things, keeping Bung’s Bond Slaves
working until 11 instead of until 9 on Saturday nights,
wero Messrs, Meagher, Kendal O’Brien, also Pat O’Brien
and Tim Harrington, the couple who are distinguishing
themselves by blocking the St. Patrick’s Day Bill in the
interest of the spongers on St. Patrick. Nannetti, the
“TLabour leader ;”’ John Redmond, the Chairman of the
Party; Crean, Doherty, Jas. O’Connor, John O’Connor,
Young, Clancy, O'Kelly, C. MacVeigh, Reddy, Shee-
han, Flavin, O’Dowd, J. Nolan, who, we understand,
travels in the business; O'Hare, Mooney, of Messrs.
Mooney, the well-known Bung’s, at whose shops it was,
if not is, impossible to get Irish-made Hop Bitters;
M‘Killop, M‘Hugh, Hogan, White, AL raham, Ambrose,
Cogan, Halpin, Waldron, “ Mr. Augustine Roche and
myself,”’ and Mr. T. M. Healy.

There was a good deal of laughter indulged in when
there was an instruction given by the Dublin Corpora-
tion to its officials to address all letters in Irish. In a
short time Buff and Alf Foz may be laughing with the
wrong side of their mouths. There are a large number
of carnest Irish Irelanders in the offices of the Dublin
Corporation, and they may be relied upon to carry out
the instructions. A few days ago the Electric Lighting
Department had occasion to write to our office, and the
envelope is addressed as follows:—

An Raname,
“Tae LEADER,
33 Sparo na Mamrepeac, 10¢Tatyy,
Daite &cd Cuat.

Alf Foz has been, if we may very much mix a meta-
phor, in its clephantine way, trying to make fun out of
the report that the name of one Vance is to be Irishised
Danp, or something in that way. We wondér how v 11
the officials Trishise 4/f Foz should they have occasion
to address a letter to that sporting gentleman?

After Tallaght—Buttevant, in the County of Cork.
His Highness Sergeant Sullivan is departing from
Buttevant, and there was a loyal and dutiful meeting
at the Dispensary on May 23rd. The following circular
was the result :— Dear Sir (or Madam)—At a xrepre-
sontative meeting, held at the Dispensary, Buttevant,
on May 23rd, it was resolved to present Sergeant Sulli-
van, on the occasion of his departure from Buttevant,
with a suitable acknowledgment of the able and valued
services which he rendered to the people of the town
and district while stationed at Buttevant. In order to
give his many friends and admirers an opportunity of
co-operating in tho movement, a public subscription list
was' opened. Should you be pleased to participate in
this worthy project a subscription would be gratefully
recoived and duly acknowledged by Dr. Corbett, Hon.
Treas., or by L. Tully, Hon Sec.” Rebel Cork an ead!
‘Whon bobbies give “ able and valued services ” to a Cork
town, where’s the use of Cork making itself ridiculous
by the grotesque adjective “rebel?””  Perhaps some
Buttevant reader of ours would give us interesting in-
formation concerning this nation-making scheme
hatched in the Dispensary of Buttevant.

There was a something called a ¢ Lend a Hand Club
recently founded in Dublin. A certain “Idolatrous’

lady was anxious to lend a hand, and communicated with
the club. Some time afterwards she received a letter
from one of the lady honorary officers of the club:—
* We are very anxious to get a few members to join in
going to the S. D. Union on Sunday afternoons to help
in singing hymns. Some ladies go every-Sunday to read
Pprayers in the infirmary wards, and after that they sing
a few hymns. Do you think you could help in this?
Even once a fortnight, or month, if every Sunday would
be too much. The prayers begin at 3 o’clock, and the
singing is at about 3.30, I believe.”” Is this a branch
of the Salvation Army or what? Or is it a proselytising
dodge? Onme would have thought that the energies of
the “ Lend a Hand Club ’’ would go more in the direc-
tion of helping on the material lot of those needing
help. We assume that there is a Catholic and a Protes-
tant chaplain connected with the S. D. Unien, and if
the spiritual needs of the Protestant inmates are not
looked after, surely there are plenty of professedly Pro-
testant organisations that can send people, as Protes-
tants, to do what may be needed. But the ““Lend a
Hand Club”’ is a non-sectarian body, we understand.
Tt was started under the patronage—or, anyway, with
the help—of Lady Aberdeen, and we believe that
Monsignor Molloy, an aristocratic Catholic clergyman,
also had something to do with the founding of it. We
would like to hear more about this “Lend a Hand
Club.”

We congratulate Seamup O Ueipn , the author of
the well-known propagandist play, “An "Oocedip,’” on
his election as medical officer to the Oughterard Dis-
pensary District.

It will be remembered that the Guardians stipulated
in their advertisement that applicants should have a
knowledge of Irish. Seamup O Deinn has a knowledge
of Trish, and the other applicant, Dr. O’Brien, stated
e that he was studying Irish, and that he hoped he would
be able to converse in it in a short time. On the face
of it, the latter candidate did not comply with one of
the stipulations set forth in the advertisement, and so
there was in reality only one eligible candidate for the
vacancy. The Chairman said:—So long as a doctor
is a doctor in this Union, he ought to know TIrish and
be able to understand what the people say. But we
have inserted an advertisement for an Irish-speaking
% doctor, and I say we must go by the terms of the adver-
tisement. And I say there is only one application be-
fore me that complies with the terms of the advertise-
ment.”” The Chairman ruled out of order a motion in
favour of Dr. O’Brien, and declared Seamup O Denn
elected.

A strange story reaches us from Cahirciveen, in the
kingdom of Kerry. The Kerry County Instructress in
Cookery is, or has been, holding classes in Cahirciveen.
This young lady, who, by the way, it is understood is a
member of a Cork branch of the Gaelic League, is not
popular with Irish Irelanders in Cahirciveen. The tribe
known as “ nominal Home Rulers’’ are familiar in his-
tory; we fear there are a good many nominal Gaclic
Leaguers strolling round these days. This young lady,
Miss O’Callaghan, is, we understand, an employé of the
Kerry County Council, so that we may expect that the
grievance which fourteen young ladies have against her
will be promptly looked to. The least that the Cookery
Instructress can be asked to do is to apologise to the
young ladies, and to remove the obstruction which she
placed to their attending her classes.

The secretary of the local branch of the Gz&chc League
18 T. MacDonncan Ua MaodSamny, a pr_ommeu(’. figure
in the Irish Ireland fight, who, the public may remem-
ber, sacrificed the Commission of the Peace in a tussle

how to behave to Irish women of spirit in future. We
are glad that this incident has occurred, for we I_eel

vice to the Irish movement.

We sco by the Report for 1905 of the Youghal Co-
operative Lace Society that the receipts for the year
were £3,658 15s. 5d., and the wages paid to the workers
amounted to £3,519 13s. 6d. The nuns of the Presen-
tation Convent direct this industry. On the cccasion
of the annual meeting several appropriate mottoes in
TIrish hung upon the walls in the convent, and the pro- 3 E
ceedings wound up with an entertainment, which in- r N
cluded a lullaby in Irish and a chorus, “ Hymn to St. ’
Patrick,”” an old Irish air arranged by Rev. G. O’Neill,
S.J.

Rev. Dr. Hogan’s book, entitled “ Irish Catholics and
Trinity College,’”' makes very interesting reading; and
that is a great deal to say at this time of day of any-
thing concerning tho scemingly eternal University ques-
tion. We are somewhat tired of this question; it is
all talk and no fighting; it is associated with too much
logic chopping and too little head chopping for our
taste. The case has been proved over'and over again ;
ethics have done their work, but the Irish Catholics i
refuse to bite. Wo have asked and asked in vain for )
a list for publication of the Nutting scholars, who are
Catholics. If these Nutting scholars had any pluck
they would publish their own names, but the hugger-
mugger concerning them plainly shows that they
sneaked, rather than walked boldly, into Trinity.

In the Preface Dr. Hogan says: — Although, like
all Trish Catholics, I feel strongly on the subject, I have,
nevertheless, done my best to keep within the bounds
of cowmrtzous and civil controversy. If I have not suc-
ceeded as perfectly as my opponents might wish, I would
ask‘ them to believe that it is not through any wanton
desire of wounding their feelings.””  Tho italics are
ows. And candidly we do not like the tolerance-prov-
ng spirit that they emphasise. ~ What does it matter
to anyone in a fair fight what his oppouents think of
him?  The object of honourable fighting is to beat your
opponent fairly. The Irish Catholics, as a body, though
strong in intellect, are yet weak in the backbone. We
would gladly take 25 per cent. off the intellect of Ivich
Catholics if we could add 25 ver cent. to tho strength
of the “Idolatrous’ backbone.

Dr. Hogan’s pamphlet is very readable and interest-

when all has been said and done, the real need is not

The Wesleyan Soldiers’ Home, Curragh Camp, want
a “ Male Cook,”” and he must be “Saved.”” If he were
not ““ Saved,’” perhaps the Wesleyanism of this Tommies’
Home might be undermined! The advertisement runs
as follows : —“ Male Cook Wanted, single; Protestant;
strictly sober; plain cooking only; well up in buns and
pastry. Apply, with references (copies only), stating
age and wages required, to Manager, Wesleyan Soldiers’
Home, Curragh Camp.”” Poor man! as well as having
to be one of the “ Saved *’ he must be “ well up in buns
and pastry,”’ but he must be “ down,’” as it were, in the
beer line.

At the last meeting of the Waterford County Coun-
cil, held at Dungarvan, Mr, Michael Power proposed :
“That on and after January 1st, 1907, an elememtary
knowledge of Irish shall be required from all candidates
seeking any position in the gift of the Council. That
a committee be appointed to draw up a programme and
arrange other details.”” That, certainly, is a mild and
sweetly reasonable resolution to put before the County
Council of one of the largely Irish-speaking coun-

ties Mr. O'Connor supported it, and did so
in Irvish. Father Michael Walsh, C.C., of Dun-
garvan, appeared with a deputation from the local
branch ¢l the Gaelic League, and addressed the Coun-

cil in favour
over, opposed

f the resolution. Mr. M. Walsh, how-
Mr. M. Walsh said that he always
spoke Irish i home and encouraged the speaking of
it, “but it is not the best men who are the best Irish
speakers.” And what, pray, had that to do with a
resolution demanding that all candidates for positions
in the gift of the Council should have an elemeniary
knowledge of Irish on and after January Ist next? If
they applied the rule to the Irish Parliamentary Party,
argued by Mr. Walsh, the two Redmonds and a large
number of others would be put out of it! Well, cer-
tainly Mr. Walsh has a very poor estimate of the
brains of the two Redmonds. We certainly would be
sorry to think that if either John or William Redmond
got six months to prepare, that they would not be able
to acquire an elemeniary knowledge of Irish. By the
way, we have a recollection of reading somewhere that
Mr. W. Redmond was studying Irish, and no doubt he
has already at least an elementary knowledge of it.
Mr. O’Gorman thought that the demand for an ele-
mentary knowledge of Irish from all applicants was
going too far, and suggested that a preference for those
knowing Irish, other things being equal, would be sufli-
cient. The amendment suggested by Mr. O’Gorman
was carried by 10 votes to 6. In view of Mr. M.
Walsh’s hostile attitude to Mr, Michael Power's
proposal, it is strange to find that Mr. Walsh is one of
the Treasurers to the John Fleming Testimonial ; he was
at one time a pupil of John Fleming.

A correspondent of ours, in the course of a note con-
cerning the patronage of Irish-made bicycles, says:—
But the writer of this, with some assistance, had an
opportunity of testing the veracity and accuracy of such
popular belief on Sunday last by tho simple device of
inspecting some eight dozen machines on the grounds
where a Gaelic football match was in progress in Kinne-
gad Parish, just outside Mullingar. Needless to say,
the task of inspection, and enumeration, although
simple, was a very discouraging one, especially from the
point of view of the Industrial Revival, for only two
bicycles of Trish manufacture were actually found in
the eight dozen. The others were all English- made,
oven last season, and this season’s machines, so that
there appears to be no tendency to abandon foreign in
favour of Irish manufacture on the part of these Gaels,
and, doubtless, the same account might bs given if a
similar inspection were tried on other football grounds
in the midlands. But it will be very interesting to find
if the same tale can be told of the south and west.”

This is the Committes of Management of a ‘Working-

Irish Temperance Executive. We want to know who
are the Trustees of this club.”

On Whit Monday, at the Elm Park Ground, Lea
Bridge Road, there will be what, for short, we may call
a London-Irish Feip. The athletic section is being
organised by the G.A.A., and the other sections by the
London Gaelic League. This note is by way of reminder
to our London readers. The proceedings will commence
at 12 noon.

in a shop—Is this Irish manufacture? We don’t know ;
but then we are only clod-hoppers in comedy compared

R. THOMAS O’DONNELL did a good stroke of

work on “the floor of the House’ for the

status of Irish in the Intermediate. Many members are

also doing good work with the object of establishing a

system of competitive examination for clerkships, by
blocking the Great Northern Bill.

Now, the action of Mr. Thomas O’Donnell, concern-
ing the status of Irish in the Intermediate, and the
blocking of the Great Northern Railway Bill, were led
up to by the work done at home in Ireland in the carry-
ing out of what we may call the LEADER or Sinn féin
policy.  The great and complex activities of the lan-
guage movement, and the chequered but dogged struggle
for Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play, .were necessary
preliminaries for the doing of anything effectivatin both
connections\“ on the floor of the House.”” Irish Ireland
might resolute and clamour in vain at the Intermediate
Commissioners if a vote of the “ House’ did not pull
the latter up; and the efforts of Mr. M. Sullivan, Mr.
Dolan and others at the last general meeting of the
Great Northern Railway, following up in their turn the
protracted newspaper agitation, could be laughed at, did
not “ the floor of the House’ provide machinery for
blocking the Bill of the notorious Railway Co. Whether
the action “on the floor’’ will, or will not, secure the
establishment of the competitive examinations in con-
nection with the clerkships of thé Great Northern Rail-
way, we cannot yob say, but certainly the action of the
various members who have taken up the matter has
carried on the movement a considerable step forward.

There may be people who get tired of our persistence
in tracking down bigotry and striving for tolerance, and
who are disappointed that we do not give them a new
thrill every month. However, this paper was estab-

of trifling readers on the look out for new sensations
and novelty in the matter of thrills or tittle-tattle. If
there are any readers who have got tired of our
attack on the intolerance, under which so many of the

HENRIK IBSEN.
I_I ENRIK IBSEN has died, leaving not his like

behind, savo in so far as he has left it in his

works. I am not going to write a rhapsody on Ibsen;
I simply think him entitled to a slightly more intelligent
eritique than that of the Freeman’s Journal. He in-
fluenced the modern drama, and the modern stage, more
than any other man of his day, and perhaps some of his
influence may he seen even in some of the plays of our
Anglo-Trish writers. For this reason, if for no other,
thero is some need to notice him and his work,
The dramatic work of the Norwegian playwright falls
into at least two parts; his earlier work, which was
largely derivative, and his later, which was original. It
is by the latter that Western Europe knows him best,
and it is this which I intend noticing. Some think that
his great poetical dramas claim the higher place, and
possibly they do; but as they have neither challenged
the same notice, nor exerted the same influence as his
prose dramas of latter-day life, I shall not deal with
them here. These prose dramas are the things most
characteristic of Ibsen, and the most original contribu-
tion he has made to the dramaturgy of the last century.
It has been said of Euripides that he brought the tragic
drama down nearer to the level of ordinary life than
did either of his two great predecessors. bsen did
something of the same kind for the modern prose drama
—only more so. He wrote plays, all or much of which
you could fancy really happening, or, at all events, as
possibly happening. Now, in a play like T. W. Robert-
son’s delightful Caste, you feel—if you so far suspend
your enjoyment as to think about the play at all—that
what you see on the stage is not what happens in real
life. Rather, it is something that pleases your imagi-
nation, tickles your humour, gently thrills your emo-

tion, and in general appeals to what may
be called the romance-sense—a faculty which
none of wus, I hope, are so poor as to be

quite destitute of. When you think the matter over,
you begin to suspect that these were just the effects
Robertson aimed at producing. Possibly, too, he meant
to edify you as well as please you, but you feel that he
meant to please you, at all events. Not so, Ibsen; he
writes as if he didn’t care whether you were vexed or
pleased. In fact, he writes as if you were not there at
all. He does noti go out of his way—as a rule—to shock
you or vex you, but if he skould happen to rub you the
wrong way, you feel that he is likely to be rather glad
of it, possibly a bit proud of it. ~ All the old stock
cliches, conventions, traditional “ situations’’ and tell-
ing “effects ’—all these ave cast into the limbo of
vanities by Ibsen, who writes as one fully persuaded
that flat reality is good enough material for the stage.
‘With Ibsen there is no mancuvring or lying in wait to
amuse you, because he does not care whether you are
amused or not. If you want to be startled or shocked,
you may be so, if you like; he doesn’t care. He writes
to please himself, certainly to cxpress himself, so that
he is indifferent as to whether you ave pleased or not.

If Ibsen was unconventional in the matter of stage-
craft (though he was a vare adept at it), he was even
move removed from the conventional in the moral
matter of his plays. Before his time, the average play-
wright was wont to pub the virtues and vices into sepa-
rato ducts, each carrying a pure “stream of tendency **
unsullied—or unvedeomed—Dby the presence of any baser
—or nobler—matter. With the dvamatist of the old
Kotzebuo School, Virtue was a faiv river, flowing down
orystalline and undefiled to the sea of Perfect Happi-
ness.  There were never any dead dogs in that fair
river, nor any sewage matter discharged into it. There
might, indeed, be dark, wicked rocks in the way, which

239

strove to divert the fair waters from their destined
course ; but they prevailed not. The noble stream
might be fretted and chafed into rapids, but it flowed
on, triumphant, to the sea of Perfect Happiness. Vice,
on the othor hand, was a slimy sewer, full of all bad
things; never a drop of pure water from cither heaven
or carth got into it anywhere. It had not a single good
quality.  With Henrik Ibsen all this was changed. Io
seemed to picture neither virtue nor vice, but just
human shortcomings, and, occasionally, human good-
ness—of a sort. With him vice and virtue were liable
to mingle their waters together quite unexpectedly, just
as sometimes happens in real life.  Ibsen paints no
devils incarnate, but he gives you plenty of “ bad lots ;"
he pictures no blameless heroes, but he gives you average
men—as well, no doubt, as some who swerve widely
from average conduct. His women are apt to be a bit
quee: they were cyphers, or else freaks of the moods-
and-nerves order. On the whole, however, the Norwe-
glan dvamatist may fairly be said to have brought the
stage back into contact with something which, at least,
resembled the actual life of modern men.

A survey of his plays, much less an analysis of them,
is hardly to be expected here. His first prose play, 7'he
League of Youth, in my view, a brilliant comedy. I
wish we had a comedy of Irish life equal to it. We
could surely find characters like Stensgard in the world
of Irish political life and ambition.  dAn Enemy of the
People I do not rvegard as a success; it is simply a
parable in reply to his critics, and not legitimate drama
at all. The famous Doll’s House is brilliantly written,
and the psychology very true up to the point of Nora
Helmer’s “revolt.”” The petted little kitten-girl was
not the person to’ suddenly develop into an amazon
champion of “woman’s higher destiny,’” Ghosts is a
terrible play, a study of heredity. In John Gabriel
Borkmann the dramatist ingeniously brings out the
dominant point of egotism and selfishness in each of his
. characters. In Rosmersholm we have the seamy side of
the freethought world. In the Pillars of Socicty we get
a pretty good conspectus of the forces of corruption in
the trading world; the sort of things that make one
understand why Goldsmith said that “honour sinks
where commerce long prevails.”

Ibsen is the dramatist of disillusion, of moral squalor,
of pessimism. He is a social psychologist and philo-
sopher—only it is no easy thing to say what his philo-
sophy is, unless it be the conclusion come to in the
Enemy of the People, that “ The strongest man is he who
stands alone.”” Is it a sound conclusion? Surely the
strongest man is he who attracts most other men to his
side? No doubt Ibsen does well to underline the reali-
ties of life. We may amuse ourselves with romance,
and even stimulate ourselves with it, but we cannot
live on it, and if Ibsen helps to drive home this fact,
we must concede him some meed of praise. The fault
that perhaps might be most freely ‘urged against Ibsen
is that the atmosphere of his plays too often recks w1t’]1
“problems,”’ or else with pessimism. There is a “ cloge 9
air about his plays—tho air, as it were, of town life.
Ts this as it should be? Surely, no. As well as the
smoke-pall and impure atmosphere of cities there is
surely also such a thing as sunlit hills, and breezy sea-
shores. But Ibsen gives you very little hint of any such
“good airs;” he seems to keep obstinately in tho moral
purlicus all the time. However, let it bo admitted that
there is a good deal of the “ problem ’ element in real
life, and a good deal of shiftiness and sophistication in
human society to-day. After all, perhaps Ibsen’s
greatest merit is that he makes you think ; really think,
If he amused you like Congreve or Sheridan, you V{ou’ld
simply enjoy him, and not think a bit. If he thrilled,
delighted, or impressed you, like Shakespeare, you would
simply enjoy being bhl‘illied or impressed, and think very
little after all. But Ibsen fairly forces you to look at
life, and think about it as you had hardly done before.
May not this be the highest Art—or something higher
than Art} IMAAL.

“¢IRISH CATHOLICS AND TRINITY COLLEGE.”

R. HOGAN, of Maynooth, has recently published
I f ) 2 rather lengthy pamphlet dealing with the
2 Trinity Collego phase of the University Question. It

3 4 is now nearly two years since a well-known colleague of
Dr. Hogan’s advocated, before a great clerical assembly
in Maynooth, the idea of Catholics entering Trinity
College in an organised body. “There had, he said,

‘been too much useless ‘talk; and the plan he suggested

was the most practical way of putting an end to the

 University trouble. Since then that idea has been
i ground ; and it is to those who champion it that
an’s new ook is specially addressed. Bub Dr.

more generous than his Dunboyne friend. ’l_
¥ in justice, ours—so much he .concedes, and, in-

cod, establishes. But he would have us make a present of

it to the Protestants, and look for another University
urselves.

;n&: maj; be well to look at this idea nakedly before
ining Dr. Hogan’s treatment of it. Wo Irish
tholics pay enormous sums of moncy for the upkeep
versity in the advantages of which we take no

0 have made an unwilling present of this Uni-

THE LEADER.

versity to’a very small and a very obnoxious portion of
the community.  These Protestants do not want Catho-
lics to have any share in the good things that have been
given them by the Catholics. But at tho same time
they know that, if Catholics once thought of having a
share, Protestants might say good-bye to the whole; for
they could never compete with the Catholic mind in
educational matters. So that, in order to keep the
Catholic from coming within stone-throw of them, they
set up a form of screeching and howling thab is cal-
culated to keep the Catholics away. Trinity, therefore,
is tho big dog with the bone beneath its paws, growling
and looking around treacherously as it munges the
stolen farc; and we do not grapple with it for our
rights, but scamper away with our tails down, and take
up a whining attitude of please-sir-/Le-took-all-our-bone-
and-we-want-another.  That is plainly our position.
And it is well that we should own to it if we are to
remedy it or to justify it. .

Dr. Hogan would justify it. To him the Trinity
animal is a dreadful brute. It would tear us asunder,
the Maynooth Professor thinks, and convert our flesh
into the material of its own unholy carcass, if we made
the slightest attempt to beard it in its den. Dr. Hogan
may be right or he may be wrong. Here I am con-
cerned only with the arguments he has advanced for his
opinion in the pages of his new book.

It is the object of his book, he says, “ to bring before
the minds of the Catholic public the full force of the
Protestant influences that are at work in Trinity Col-
lege, to show how deceptive is the hope that Catholics
might, if they only went there in sufficient numbers,
one day get a position in the establishment commen-
surate with their numbers and their ability, and how
complzctely they would be in the meantime at the mercy
of the full tide of Protestantism that flows around its
walls.””  To making good the statement contained in
the first member of this sentence, Dr. Hogan devotes
some 100 quarto pages. He shows how Trinity is, as a
matter of fact, aggressively anti-Catholic in its constitu-
tion and spirit. For this part of his book most of us
will, T daresay, be thankful. But when Dr. Hogan
goes on to establish the remaining—and, indeed, the
main—portion of his Quod Erat Demonstrandwm, he
takes a big logical—or rather an illogical—jump, and
says : —Therefore, since Trinity is so bitterly anti-
Catholic, there is no hope of Catholics overthrowing its
constitution and spirit if they went there in sufficient
numbers. Now it is one thing to make clear that, as it
stands at present, both in its constitution and in its
spirit Trinity is aggressively anti-Catholic; but it is
something quite different to make clear that Trinity
could withstand an invasion of Catholic spirit and re-
main unchanged. Yet Dr. Hogan- lays: down the
latter as a sort of necessary conclusion from the former.
Here surely his logic is at fault, and because of -this
essential defect his book must be reckoned a failure.

Still, a carveful reader will not find it difficult to under-
stand how Dr. Hogan could have .fallen into so' grave
an error. The Maynooth Professor has no faith in the
fighting powers of the averago Irish Catholic, while it
is clear that he has been awe-stricken with something—
and for the life of me I cannot think what it may be—
in Protestantism. Ilere are a few sentences from 'his
book : —

Tn #he Proface he writes:— T desire, ‘therefore, to
say now from the outset what I have said a few times
elsewhere in these pages, ithat I have not been animated
by the slightest personal ill-will towards any of the dis-
tinguished personages from whose works and speeches I
have quoted extracts, or whose general attitudetowards
Catholicism I have endeavoured to make clear.”

On pago 31 he writes : —“ We do not grudge the Pro-
testants of Treland all the advantages of education, all
the blessings of a great and powerful school. We are
willing, for the sake of national harmony, that they
should retain in peace and comfort even the endowments
of our confiscated property. ‘We know quite well that
they are destined to play a great part still in the life of
the nation. i

Surely Dr. Hogan cannot mean to be serious—or, if

he does, he must be a very ethereal sort of creature—
when he says that he bears no ill-will towards the bigots
from whose anti-Catholic tirades ho has quoted in his
pamphlet. But it is quite evident that he is afraid of
the Protestants. For surely if ho were in his wits he
could not, at this hour of day, have suspected the Pro-
testants of being destined to play a great part in the lifo
of this_ nation. ~That sort of thing might well make one
laugh if it were not sad to think of it as coming from
a Maynooth Professor. And after all T am not so suro
that we should have minded it much if Dr. Hogan were
not _gmlt_v of a graver and a more unpardonable crror.
He is a Maynooth Professor, a Doctor of Divinity, and
one would think that he, above others, might feel the
strength of the Catholic cause.

But he really seems to think that Catholicism ne
sarily carries with it a weakness of character which
makes it unable to withstand reproach—that in itself
and as a system of doctrine it is poorly set together,
and very weak in many of its parts. For if he did not
think this, how could he have written thus?:—

*“To all those who suggest that Trinity College should
be invaded, stormed, captured, I respectfully submit
that the struggle would be so protracted, the attack so
bitterly contested, the odds against us so overwhelming
that, even were a lodgment of some kind ultimately
effected in the fortress, our soldiers would come out of
the engagement so demoralised that victory would
scarcely be less fatal than defeat.’””

“I need say nothing here of the many opportunitics
a Professor of Astronomy has of discrediting the Church
in its long and varied relations with the science which he
teaches. The Paschal controversy, the Anatolian Cycle,
the Gregorian Calendar, the invention of telescopes,
the rise and influence of astrologers, the persecution of
Keppler, the trial of Galileo—these are surely questions
which afford a Professor of Astronomy an opening if he
chooses to take advantage of it.

“But the Professor of Political Economy has still
more numerous and more practical opportunities of
doing us harm. ¥

“And when we bear in mind that the science of
medicine is penetrated through and through with all
the problems to which modern biology has given rise,
o we see at once that ample opportunity is
offered for bringing before the student questions, which,
if not approached with due regard for supernatural
faith, easily make havoc of the creed of a Catholic.”

I cannot bring myself to protest against writing such
as that—T shall only say that it is the sort of thing the
Sourfaces like. But, whatever Dr. Hogan may think,
the day of the Sourface ascendancy is over, and we do
not begrudge him the little favourable breath with
which he tries to fan the last flicker of a candle that is

Lot me go further. Suppose at the next BAIa_vnnrJth
meeting the Bishops were to make up their minds that
the only course now to be adopted in the University
trouble was the invasion of Trinity. Suppose that, in
accordance with that idea, a number of the clever boys
from the Catholic Secondary Schools were sent to Trinity
at the opening of next term. They would know that they
wera going into the enemy’s camp, and that they were
to have their armour on. They would know that they
stood for a great cause, and they would certainly prove
themselves equal to it. They would not be the sort of

structions, and giving their adoration, in Stephen’s

such men would fall a prey to the Protestantism of Tri-
nity? Of course, it may be urged that Catholics have.
as a matter of fact, suffered shipwreck in the faith by
going to Trinity. That may be true. And if it is, T
can casily understand it. For these men were deserters
from the ranks, and one would expect little better from
them. But in the case which I am supposing matters
